← Dong Liu · Research

Technical Note · July 2026 · Draft, Comments Welcome

Fourier Transform Option Pricing: From Characteristic Functions to Heston PFE

傅立叶变换期权定价：从特征函数到Heston框架下的PFE与Range Digital

✎ Dong Liu 📅 July 2026 🔗 LinkedIn 💬 Comments welcome

Abstract

The Black-Scholes formula is a special case of a more general pricing framework: whenever the risk-neutral density of the underlying is unknown but its characteristic function has closed form, the Carr–Madan (1999) Fourier transform allows a single Fast Fourier Transform call to price an entire strike curve. This note derives the transform from first principles, starting from the risk-neutral pricing identity applied to Black-Scholes, then generalizes to Heston stochastic volatility and Merton jump-diffusion, verifying at each step that the framework collapses back to the closed-form Black-Scholes price when the characteristic function is that of a normal distribution.

We then apply the machinery to two problems of direct relevance to counterparty credit risk and structured product desks. First, Potential Future Exposure (PFE) under Heston dynamics, where the state includes not just spot but instantaneous variance, requiring a two-dimensional (tenor × variance) precomputed FFT grid combined with the Andersen (2008) quadratic-exponential scheme for path simulation, since realistic equity parameters violate the Feller condition. Second, range digital options, whose Carr–Madan transform is derivable from the same machinery with one fewer order in the damping polynomial, and whose price is shown to be considerably more sensitive to the choice of stochastic volatility model than a vanilla option’s, due to the interplay of skew and volatility-of-volatility on the shape of the terminal density.

Keywords: Carr-Madan transform, characteristic function, Fast Fourier Transform, Heston model, Merton jump-diffusion, Potential Future Exposure, quadratic-exponential scheme, range digital option

Contents

  1. From Black-Scholes to Characteristic Functions
  2. The Carr–Madan Transform and FFT Discretization
  3. Beyond Black-Scholes: Heston and Merton Jump-Diffusion
  4. Numerical Verification
  5. Application I: PFE Simulation Under Heston
  6. Application II: Range Digital Options
  7. Practical Limits and Scope
  8. References

I. From Black-Scholes to Characteristic Functions

1.1 The Risk-Neutral Pricing Identity

Every European option price reduces to a discounted expectation under the risk-neutral measure $\mathbb{Q}$:

$$C(K,T) = e^{-rT}\,\mathbb{E}^{\mathbb{Q}}\!\left[(S_T-K)^+\right] = e^{-rT}\int_{\ln K}^{\infty}(e^s-K)\,q(s)\,ds,\tag{1}$$

where $q(s)$ is the risk-neutral density of $X_T=\ln S_T$. Computing (1) requires knowing $q(s)$ explicitly. Under Black-Scholes, $X_T\sim N\big(\ln S_0+(r-\tfrac12\sigma^2)T,\ \sigma^2T\big)$ is normal, and the integral in (1) can be evaluated in closed form, this is the origin of the familiar $S_0N(d_1)-Ke^{-rT}N(d_2)$ formula. Under any other model of the underlying’s dynamics (stochastic volatility, jumps), $q(s)$ generally has no closed form, and (1) cannot be integrated directly.

1.2 The Characteristic Function as a Substitute for the Density

The characteristic function of $X_T$ is the Fourier transform of its density:

$$\phi(u) = \mathbb{E}^{\mathbb{Q}}\!\left[e^{iuX_T}\right] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{iux}\,q(x)\,dx.\tag{2}$$

Because the Fourier transform is invertible, $\phi(u)$ and $q(x)$ carry exactly the same information, in principle $q(x)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int e^{-iux}\phi(u)\,du$. The practical value of this equivalence is that for many models of interest (Heston, Merton, Variance Gamma, CGMY, and others), $\phi(u)$ has a simple closed-form expression even though $q(x)$ does not. Carr and Madan’s (1999) insight was to price options directly from $\phi(u)$ via a damped Fourier transform, sidestepping the need for $q(x)$ altogether.

Black-Scholes characteristic function

For $X_T=\ln S_T$ under the Black-Scholes SDE with initial log-price $x_0=\ln S_0$ and drift $\mu=r-q-\tfrac12\sigma^2$:

$$\phi_{BS}(u) = \exp\!\left\{iu\big(x_0+\mu T\big) - \tfrac12u^2\sigma^2T\right\}.\tag{3}$$

This follows directly from the moment generating function of a normal random variable, $\mathbb{E}[e^{iu\sigma W_T}]=e^{-\frac12u^2\sigma^2T}$. Equation (3) is the object we substitute into the general Carr–Madan formula in Section II to verify that it reduces exactly to the closed-form BS price.

II. The Carr–Madan Transform and FFT Discretization

2.1 Why the Call Price Itself Cannot Be Fourier-Transformed

Write the call price as a function of log-strike $k=\ln K$: $C(k)=e^{-rT}\int_k^{\infty}(e^s-e^k)\,q(s)\,ds$. As $k\to-\infty$ (deep in-the-money), $C(k)\to S_0$, a nonzero constant. A function that does not decay at infinity is not integrable ($\int|C(k)|\,dk=\infty$), so its Fourier transform does not exist. The fix is a damping factor.

2.2 Damping and the Transform of the Damped Price

Define $c_T(k)=e^{\alpha k}C(k)$ for a damping coefficient $\alpha>0$, chosen so that $e^{\alpha k}\to 0$ fast enough as $k\to-\infty$ to make $c_T(k)$ integrable. Its Fourier transform is

$$\psi(u) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{iuk}\,c_T(k)\,dk.$$

Substituting the definition of $C(k)$ and swapping the order of integration (justified by Fubini for $\alpha>0$) reduces the double integral to an elementary one, yielding the central result of Carr and Madan (1999):

Proposition 1: Carr–Madan transform

$$\psi(u) = \frac{e^{-rT}\,\phi\big(u-(\alpha+1)i\big)}{\alpha^2+\alpha-u^2+i(2\alpha+1)u}.\tag{4}$$

The call price is recovered by inverse Fourier transform and removing the damping factor:

$$C(k) = \frac{e^{-\alpha k}}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\mathrm{Re}\!\left[e^{-iuk}\psi(u)\right]du.\tag{5}$$

Equation (4) shows that the option price is expressible purely in terms of the characteristic function evaluated at the complex point $u-(\alpha+1)i$, no knowledge of the density is required. Substituting the Black-Scholes $\phi_{BS}(u)$ from (3) into (4)–(5) and integrating numerically reproduces the closed-form BS price to high precision, which is the standard sanity check for any implementation of this framework.

2.3 Discretization: From Continuous Integral to Discrete FFT

Truncate the integral in (5) at $u_j=j\eta$ for $j=0,\dots,N-1$ and place a grid of log-strikes $k_m=-b+\lambda m$, $b=N\lambda/2$. Substituting and requiring the exponential kernel to match the standard DFT form $e^{-i\frac{2\pi}{N}jm}$ forces the constraint

$$\eta\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{N},\tag{6}$$

which is the single most important practical constraint of the method: frequency-domain resolution $\eta$ and strike-domain resolution $\lambda$ cannot be chosen independently. With (6) satisfied, the discretized integral becomes a standard DFT,

$$C(k_m) \approx \frac{e^{-\alpha k_m}}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Re}\!\left[\sum_{j=0}^{N-1} e^{-i\frac{2\pi}{N}jm}\,x_j\right],\tag{7}$$

$$x_j = e^{ijb\eta}\,\psi(u_j)\,\frac{\eta}{3}\big[3+(-1)^{j+1}-\delta_{j,0}\big],$$

where the trailing bracket is a Simpson-rule quadrature weight, standard practice for improving accuracy over naive trapezoidal integration. A single FFT call on the sequence $\{x_j\}$ (cost $O(N\log N)$) then returns the entire price curve $\{C(k_m)\}_{m=0}^{N-1}$ across all strikes simultaneously, this is the source of the method’s practical advantage over strike-by-strike numerical integration.

III. Beyond Black-Scholes: Heston and Merton Jump-Diffusion

Because the FFT engine of Section II requires nothing but a characteristic function, extending it to richer models is a matter of substitution, not re-derivation.

3.1 Heston Stochastic Volatility

Under the Heston (1993) model,

$$dS_t = (r-q)S_t\,dt + \sqrt{v_t}\,S_t\,dW_t^1,\tag{8}$$

$$dv_t = \kappa(\theta-v_t)\,dt+\xi\sqrt{v_t}\,dW_t^2,\qquad dW_t^1dW_t^2=\rho\,dt,$$

the characteristic function of $\ln S_T$ has a closed form in terms of $(v_0,\kappa,\theta,\xi,\rho)$. We use the Gatheral (2006) “little trap” parameterization, which avoids branch-cut discontinuities in the complex square root that plague the original Heston (1993) formula for long maturities and high vol-of-vol.

Heston characteristic function (little-trap form)

With $a=\kappa\theta$, $b=\kappa$, and $d=\sqrt{(\rho\xi iu-b)^2+\xi^2(iu+u^2)}$, $g=\dfrac{b-\rho\xi iu-d}{b-\rho\xi iu+d}$:

$$\phi_{H}(u) = \exp\!\left\{C(u)+D(u)v_0+iux_0\right\},\tag{9}$$

$$C(u)=(r-q)iuT+\frac{a}{\xi^2}\!\left[(b-\rho\xi iu-d)T-2\ln\frac{1-ge^{-dT}}{1-g}\right],$$

$$D(u)=\frac{b-\rho\xi iu-d}{\xi^2}\cdot\frac{1-e^{-dT}}{1-ge^{-dT}}.$$

3.2 Merton Jump-Diffusion

Merton (1976) adds a compound Poisson jump component to the diffusion, with jump intensity $\lambda$ and lognormal jump size $\ln(1+Y)\sim N(\mu_j,\sigma_j^2)$:

$$\phi_{M}(u) = \exp\!\left\{iu\big(x_0+\text{drift}\cdot T\big)-\tfrac12\sigma^2u^2T + \lambda T\!\left(e^{iu\mu_j-\frac12\sigma_j^2u^2}-1\right)\right\},\tag{10}$$

where the drift includes a compensator $\text{drift}=r-q-\tfrac12\sigma^2-\lambda\big(e^{\mu_j+\frac12\sigma_j^2}-1\big)$ that ensures $S_t$ remains a $\mathbb{Q}$-martingale once jumps are added, the exact analogue of the $-\tfrac12\sigma^2$ correction in ordinary geometric Brownian motion, generalized to account for the expected jump size.

What each parameter controls: in Heston, $\rho<0$ (the empirically standard leverage effect) produces a downward-sloping implied volatility skew; $\xi$ (vol-of-vol) controls the curvature of the smile independently of its slope. In Merton, $\lambda$ and $\sigma_j$ jointly control the excess kurtosis, and a negative $\mu_j$ (crashes more common than rallies) produces skew from a mechanism entirely distinct from Heston’s correlation channel. This distinction matters directly for model risk: instruments sensitive to higher moments of the terminal distribution can price very differently under models calibrated to the same vanilla smile (see Section VI).

IV. Numerical Verification

Substituting $\phi_{BS}(u)$ from (3) into the FFT engine of (7) and comparing against the closed-form BS price validates the entire framework: any implementation error in the damping, discretization, or Simpson weighting would show up as a discrepancy here that a direct comparison against a known analytical answer can catch immediately.

Figure 1. Left: call price curves under Black-Scholes, Heston ($\rho=-0.7$), and Merton jump-diffusion, calibrated to comparable ATM volatility. Right: the corresponding Black-Scholes implied volatility smiles, obtained by root-finding the BS formula against each model’s FFT-generated price at each strike. The Heston curve shows the expected downward skew from negative spot-vol correlation; the Merton curve shows a shallower, more symmetric smile driven primarily by jump kurtosis rather than skew.

In the implementation underlying Figure 1, the FFT-computed Black-Scholes price against the analytical formula differs by at most $9\times10^{-4}$ across a range of strikes from 70% to 130% moneyness, with $N=2^{12}$ FFT points, a discretization error attributable to the finite strike grid and could be reduced arbitrarily by increasing $N$ or tightening $\eta$, subject to the constraint in (6).

4.1 A Byproduct: Digital Options Come (Almost) Free

A cash-or-nothing digital call, $D(K)=e^{-rT}\,\mathbb{Q}(S_T>K)$, admits its own Carr–Madan transform, derivable by the identical method as Section 2.2 but with a payoff that has no linear slope (it is a flat step, not a ramp). The corresponding damped transform is one order simpler:

$$\zeta(u) = \frac{e^{-rT}\,\phi(u-i\alpha)}{\alpha+iu},\tag{11}$$

compared with the vanilla call’s denominator $(\alpha+iu)(\alpha+1+iu)$ in (4). This is not a coincidence: since $D(K)=-\partial C(K)/\partial K$, differentiating the payoff in the spatial domain corresponds to removing one factor of $(\beta+1)$ in the transform domain. This relationship is used directly in Section VI.

V. Application I: PFE Simulation Under Heston

5.1 Why the BS-PFE Speedup Does Not Carry Over Directly

A standard efficiency trick in exposure engines is to precompute, for a fixed remaining tenor $\tau$, an entire FFT strike curve once and reuse it via linear interpolation across thousands of Monte Carlo paths and time nodes, since the underlying homogeneity relation $C(S,K,\tau)=S\cdot c(K/S,\tau)$ lets any spot/strike pair be read off a single reference curve. Under Black-Scholes this works with no modification because $\sigma$ is constant and the FFT curve depends only on $\tau$.

Under Heston, the state of the option-pricing problem at a future exposure date includes not just $S_{t_i}$ but also the instantaneous variance $v_{t_i}$, which is itself stochastic and differs across simulated paths. A single FFT curve computed at $v_0$ is no longer valid for a path whose variance has since diffused away from $v_0$. Reusing the FFT speedup therefore requires precomputing a curve for every relevant $(\tau,v)$ combination, not just every $\tau$.

Design consequence

Build a two-dimensional precomputation grid indexed by (tenor bucket, variance bucket). For each of $n_\tau$ tenor buckets $\times$ $n_v$ variance buckets, run one FFT (cost $O(N\log N)$ each), storing the resulting strike curve. At simulation time, each path/node is priced by locating its $(\tau,v_{t_i})$ in the grid, bilinearly interpolating across the two nearest variance buckets, and reading off the price at the (homogeneity-scaled) strike, entirely without a fresh FFT call.

5.2 Path Simulation: the Andersen (2008) QE Scheme

Realistic equity Heston parameters typically violate the Feller condition $2\kappa\theta\geq\xi^2$ (in our implementation, $2\kappa\theta=0.16$ against $\xi^2=0.25$), meaning the theoretical variance process can reach zero. Naive Euler discretization of (8) can then produce negative variance, which is undefined under the square root. The Andersen (2008) quadratic-exponential (QE) scheme handles this by switching between a moment-matched noncentral-chi-square approximation (low $\psi=s^2/m^2$) and an exponential-tailed approximation with an atom at zero (high $\psi$), and augments the log-spot update with a martingale-correction term so that $\mathbb{E}[S_{t+\Delta t}\mid\mathcal{F}_t]=S_t\,e^{(r-q)\Delta t}$ holds exactly regardless of discretization error:

$$\ln S_{t+\Delta t} = \ln S_t + (r-q)\Delta t + K_0 + K_1v_t + K_2v_{t+\Delta t} + \sqrt{K_3v_t+K_4v_{t+\Delta t}}\,Z,\tag{12}$$

with $\gamma_1=\gamma_2=\tfrac12$ (central scheme) and $K_0=-\rho\kappa\theta\Delta t/\xi$, $K_1=\gamma_1\Delta t(\kappa\rho/\xi-\tfrac12)-\rho/\xi$, $K_2=\gamma_2\Delta t(\kappa\rho/\xi-\tfrac12)+\rho/\xi$, $K_3=\gamma_1\Delta t(1-\rho^2)$, $K_4=\gamma_2\Delta t(1-\rho^2)$.

5.3 Numerical Results

The example below prices PFE for a long ATM European call ($K=100$, option maturity 1.25y) at quarterly exposure dates, using $N=3{,}000$ simulated paths (daily steps) and a 12-point variance bucket grid built from the empirical range of simulated $v_{t_i}$.

Figure 2. Top-left: 300 of the 3,000 simulated $S_t$ paths under the QE scheme, with the 2.5%/97.5% quantile band showing the asymmetric spread characteristic of $\rho<0$ leverage. Top-right: the corresponding $v_t$ paths, several of which approach zero, confirming the Feller-violation scenario the QE scheme is designed to handle robustly. Bottom-left: the resulting PFE (97.5% quantile exposure) and EE (mean exposure) profiles. Bottom-right: the distribution of pricing error between the fast bucket-interpolation method and a slow ground-truth re-pricing (individual FFT call per path/node), with median relative error 0.52%.

Two features of Figure 2 are worth isolating explicitly. First, martingale-correction validity: the QE scheme with (12) reproduces $\mathbb{E}[S_T]$ against the theoretical forward to within 0.4% relative error at $N=3{,}000$ paths, a standard check that catches sign or index errors in the $K_0$–$K_4$ coefficients, which otherwise fail silently (the simulation runs and produces plausible-looking output, but with a systematic bias in the resulting exposure profile). Second, the PFE profile rises monotonically from 21.6 to 34.7 across the four quarterly nodes while EE stays essentially flat near 10.3: the growth in tail exposure is attributable almost entirely to the widening of the terminal spot distribution with $\sqrt{t}$, not to any drift in the central tendency of the position’s value.

VI. Application II: Range Digital Options

6.1 European Range Digitals vs. Path-Dependent No-Touch

The term “double digital” is used in practice for two structurally different instruments. A European range digital pays $\mathbb{1}\{K_1<S_T<K_2\}$, a payoff depending only on the terminal spot, path-independent, and directly amenable to the Carr–Madan machinery of Section II. A double no-touch (common in FX) pays based on whether $S_t$ has remained inside $(K_1,K_2)$ for the entire life of the trade, a first-passage-time problem that the static, single-maturity Fourier transform used here cannot address; it requires PDE or Wiener–Hopf methods instead. The remainder of this section concerns the European case only.

6.2 Range Digitals as a Difference of Two Digitals

Since $\mathbb{1}\{K_1<S_T<K_2\}=\mathbb{1}\{S_T>K_1\}-\mathbb{1}\{S_T>K_2\}$, the range digital price is $\text{Range}(K_1,K_2)=D(K_1)-D(K_2)$, where $D(\cdot)$ is the digital transform of equation (11). Because a single FFT call already returns $D(K)$ across the entire strike grid, any number of range digital prices for arbitrary $(K_1,K_2)$ pairs are obtained essentially for free by reading off two points and subtracting, the same batching advantage vanilla options enjoy over strike-by-strike integration.

6.3 Model Sensitivity: A Non-Monotone Effect

Because a range digital measures probability mass concentrated in a specific window rather than a tail integral, it exposes features of the terminal density that a vanilla option’s price, itself an integral over the whole right tail, tends to average out.

Figure 3. Left: reference implied volatility smiles for the same three models as Figure 1. Right: range digital price $\mathbb{Q}(K-5<S_T<K+5)$ as the window center sweeps from 75 to 125. The Heston curve exceeds Black-Scholes for window centers between roughly 100 and 118, then falls below it beyond 120, the reverse of the pattern implied by the skew’s slope alone.

Isolating the underlying digital probabilities $Q(S_T>K)=D(K)e^{rT}$ directly (rather than the range difference) confirms the crossover is not a numerical artifact: Heston’s probability mass exceeds Black-Scholes’s from $K=80$ through roughly $K=115$, then falls below it for $K\geq120$. The mechanism is that Heston’s volatility-of-volatility ($\xi$) adds excess kurtosis on top of the skew from $\rho<0$; the two effects combine to concentrate probability in the body and shoulders of the distribution while thinning the extreme tail relative to a lognormal with matched ATM volatility. A pure reading of the implied-volatility skew’s slope, which reflects only local moneyness behavior, does not by itself predict this non-monotone pattern in the underlying density.

Practical implication: range accrual notes, autocallable knock-in/knock-out barriers, and other structures with digital-like payoff kinks are considerably more sensitive to the specific choice of stochastic-volatility or jump model than vanilla options priced off the same calibrated smile. This is one reason model-risk reserves on structured desks are typically larger, in relative terms, for exotic payoffs than for vanilla flow.

VII. Practical Limits and Scope

The Carr–Madan/FFT framework as presented here has well-defined boundaries.

7.1 European, Terminal-Payoff Instruments Only

The method requires only the marginal characteristic function of $S_T$ at a single fixed maturity. Path-dependent payoffs, Asian averaging, barrier monitoring, lookback extrema, or the no-touch structure discussed in Section 6.1, require the joint or transition distribution over the whole path, which a single-maturity transform does not supply. These require Monte Carlo, PDE, or specialized transform variants (e.g., Wiener–Hopf factorization for barriers).

7.2 The Frequency–Strike Resolution Trade-off

Constraint (6), $\eta\lambda=2\pi/N$, means finer strike resolution (smaller $\lambda$) forces coarser frequency resolution (larger $\eta$), and vice versa, for fixed FFT size $N$. In practice $N$ is increased (typically to $2^{12}$–$2^{14}$) rather than trading off resolution in one domain against the other.

7.3 P-Measure vs. Q-Measure in Exposure Engines

Section V simulates paths and reprices using the same set of Heston parameters, calibrated (in principle) to the risk-neutral market. Strictly, path simulation for PFE should occur under the real-world measure $\mathbb{P}$, which in principle requires a variance risk premium adjustment to the mean-reversion parameters relative to their $\mathbb{Q}$-calibrated values. Many practical implementations elide this distinction for tractability; doing so is a simplification worth flagging explicitly rather than a limitation of the FFT engine itself.

References

Andersen, L. 2008. “Efficient Simulation of the Heston Stochastic Volatility Model.” Journal of Computational Finance 11(3): 1–42.

Carr, P., and D. Madan. 1999. “Option Valuation Using the Fast Fourier Transform.” Journal of Computational Finance 2(4): 61–73.

Gatheral, J. 2006. The Volatility Surface: A Practitioner’s Guide. Hoboken: Wiley.

Heston, S. L. 1993. “A Closed-Form Solution for Options with Stochastic Volatility with Applications to Bond and Currency Options.” Review of Financial Studies 6(2): 327–343.

Lord, R., R. Koekkoek, and D. Van Dijk. 2010. “A Comparison of Biased Simulation Schemes for Stochastic Volatility Models.” Quantitative Finance 10(2): 177–194.

Merton, R. C. 1976. “Option Pricing When Underlying Stock Returns Are Discontinuous.” Journal of Financial Economics 3(1–2): 125–144.

Appendix: Complete Python Code

The three code blocks below reproduce every numerical result and figure in this article. They correspond respectively to Sections II–IV (Carr–Madan FFT engine for BS/Heston/Merton), Section V (Heston PFE via QE scheme and variance-bucket FFT precomputation), and Section VI (range digital option pricing). Copy each block into its own .py file. Dependencies: numpy, scipy, and matplotlib.

A.1: Carr–Madan FFT Engine (BS / Heston / Merton)

Implements the characteristic functions of Section III, the generic Carr–Madan FFT pricer of Section II, and reproduces Figure 1.

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
"""
Carr-Madan FFT Option Pricing Framework -- BS / Heston / Merton Jump-Diffusion Comparison
======================================================================

Structure:
  Part 1: Black-Scholes characteristic function + FFT pricing + validation against closed-form BS formula
  Part 2: Heston stochastic volatility characteristic function + FFT pricing
  Part 3: Merton jump-diffusion characteristic function + FFT pricing
  Part 4: Comparison of pricing results / implied vol smiles across the three models
"""

import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import norm
from scipy.fft import fft
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Common market parameters
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
S0 = 100.0      # spot price
r = 0.03        # risk-free rate
q = 0.00        # dividend yield
T = 1.0         # maturity (years)

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Part 1: Black-Scholes
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def bs_char_func(u, S0, r, q, sigma, T):
    """
    Characteristic function of ln(S_T) under Black-Scholes
    phi(u) = exp{ iu*(ln S0 + (r-q-0.5*sigma^2)T) - 0.5*sigma^2*u^2*T }
    """
    x0 = np.log(S0)
    mu = r - q - 0.5 * sigma ** 2
    return np.exp(1j * u * (x0 + mu * T) - 0.5 * sigma ** 2 * u ** 2 * T)


def bs_analytic_call(S0, K, r, q, sigma, T):
    """Closed-form BS formula, used as the validation benchmark for the FFT result"""
    d1 = (np.log(S0 / K) + (r - q + 0.5 * sigma ** 2) * T) / (sigma * np.sqrt(T))
    d2 = d1 - sigma * np.sqrt(T)
    return S0 * np.exp(-q * T) * norm.cdf(d1) - K * np.exp(-r * T) * norm.cdf(d2)


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Part 2: Heston stochastic volatility model
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def heston_char_func(u, S0, r, q, T, v0, kappa, theta, xi, rho):
    """
    Heston (1993) characteristic function (Gatheral "Little Trap" parameterization,
    which avoids the complex branch-cut issue)

    dS_t = (r-q) S_t dt + sqrt(v_t) S_t dW1_t
    dv_t = kappa(theta - v_t) dt + xi sqrt(v_t) dW2_t
    dW1 dW2 = rho dt

    v0    : initial variance
    kappa : mean-reversion speed
    theta : long-run mean variance
    xi    : vol of vol
    rho   : correlation between the two Brownian motions
    """
    x0 = np.log(S0)
    a = kappa * theta
    b = kappa

    d = np.sqrt((rho * xi * 1j * u - b) ** 2 + (xi ** 2) * (1j * u + u ** 2))
    g = (b - rho * xi * 1j * u - d) / (b - rho * xi * 1j * u + d)

    exp_dT = np.exp(-d * T)
    C = (r - q) * 1j * u * T + (a / xi ** 2) * (
        (b - rho * xi * 1j * u - d) * T - 2 * np.log((1 - g * exp_dT) / (1 - g))
    )
    D = ((b - rho * xi * 1j * u - d) / xi ** 2) * ((1 - exp_dT) / (1 - g * exp_dT))

    return np.exp(C + D * v0 + 1j * u * x0)


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Part 3: Merton jump-diffusion model
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def merton_char_func(u, S0, r, q, T, sigma, lam, mu_j, sigma_j):
    """
    Merton (1976) jump-diffusion characteristic function

    dS_t/S_t = (r-q-lam*k) dt + sigma dW_t + dJ_t
    Jump size Y ~ lognormal, ln(1+Y) ~ N(mu_j, sigma_j^2)
    k = E[Y] = exp(mu_j + 0.5*sigma_j^2) - 1   (drift compensator, ensures the martingale property)

    sigma   : diffusion volatility
    lam     : Poisson jump intensity (average number of jumps per unit time)
    mu_j    : mean of log jump size
    sigma_j : standard deviation of log jump size
    """
    x0 = np.log(S0)
    k_comp = np.exp(mu_j + 0.5 * sigma_j ** 2) - 1
    drift = r - q - 0.5 * sigma ** 2 - lam * k_comp

    diffusion_part = 1j * u * (x0 + drift * T) - 0.5 * sigma ** 2 * u ** 2 * T
    jump_part = lam * T * (np.exp(1j * u * mu_j - 0.5 * sigma_j ** 2 * u ** 2) - 1)

    return np.exp(diffusion_part + jump_part)


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Carr-Madan FFT pricing engine (generic, accepts any characteristic function)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def carr_madan_fft_call(char_func, S0, r, q, T, alpha=1.5, N=2 ** 12, eta=0.25):
    """
    Generic Carr-Madan FFT call option pricer

    Inputs:
      char_func : function u -> phi(u), the characteristic function of the underlying's ln(S_T)
      alpha     : damping factor, ensures the price function is integrable, typically 1.5-2.5
      N         : number of FFT points, a power of 2
      eta       : grid spacing in the frequency domain

    Outputs:
      strikes : array of strikes
      calls   : array of corresponding call prices
    """
    lam = 2 * np.pi / (N * eta)          # grid spacing in k-space, satisfies eta*lambda = 2*pi/N
    b = N * lam / 2                       # center offset of the k-space grid

    j = np.arange(N)
    u = j * eta                           # frequency-domain grid u_j = j*eta

    # Carr-Madan core formula: psi(u) = exp(-rT) * phi(u - (alpha+1)i) / (alpha^2+alpha-u^2 + i(2alpha+1)u)
    complex_u = u - (alpha + 1) * 1j
    phi_val = char_func(complex_u)
    denom = (alpha ** 2 + alpha - u ** 2) + 1j * (2 * alpha + 1) * u
    psi = np.exp(-r * T) * phi_val / denom

    # Simpson-rule weighting
    simpson_w = (3 + (-1) ** (j + 1) - (j == 0).astype(float)) / 3
    x = np.exp(1j * u * b) * psi * eta * simpson_w

    fft_vals = fft(x)
    k_grid = -b + lam * j                 # log-strike grid
    call_prices = (np.exp(-alpha * k_grid) / np.pi) * fft_vals.real

    strikes = np.exp(k_grid)
    return strikes, call_prices


def get_prices_at_strikes(char_func, target_strikes, S0, r, q, T, alpha=1.5, N=2 ** 12, eta=0.25):
    """Linearly interpolate the FFT-generated grid to read off the price at target strikes"""
    strikes_grid, calls_grid = carr_madan_fft_call(char_func, S0, r, q, T, alpha, N, eta)
    order = np.argsort(strikes_grid)
    return np.interp(target_strikes, strikes_grid[order], calls_grid[order])


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Part 1 validation: FFT vs. closed-form BS formula
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
if __name__ == "__main__":

    print("=" * 70)
    print("Part 1: Black-Scholes -- FFT pricing vs. closed-form formula, validation")
    print("=" * 70)

    sigma_bs = 0.20
    test_strikes = np.array([70, 80, 90, 100, 110, 120, 130])

    bs_cf = lambda u: bs_char_func(u, S0, r, q, sigma_bs, T)
    fft_prices_bs = get_prices_at_strikes(bs_cf, test_strikes, S0, r, q, T)
    analytic_prices_bs = np.array([bs_analytic_call(S0, K, r, q, sigma_bs, T) for K in test_strikes])

    print(f"{'K':>8}{'FFT price':>14}{'Analytic BS':>14}{'Abs diff':>14}")
    for K, fp, ap in zip(test_strikes, fft_prices_bs, analytic_prices_bs):
        print(f"{K:>8.1f}{fp:>14.6f}{ap:>14.6f}{abs(fp - ap):>14.2e}")

    max_err = np.max(np.abs(fft_prices_bs - analytic_prices_bs))
    print(f"\nMax absolute error: {max_err:.2e}  (validates that the Carr-Madan framework, "
          f"fed the BS characteristic function, reduces exactly to the BS formula)\n")

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    print("=" * 70)
    print("Part 2: Heston stochastic volatility model -- FFT pricing")
    print("=" * 70)

    heston_params = dict(v0=0.04, kappa=2.0, theta=0.04, xi=0.5, rho=-0.7)
    heston_cf = lambda u: heston_char_func(u, S0, r, q, T, **heston_params)
    fft_prices_heston = get_prices_at_strikes(heston_cf, test_strikes, S0, r, q, T)

    print(f"Parameters: v0={heston_params['v0']}, kappa={heston_params['kappa']}, "
          f"theta={heston_params['theta']}, xi={heston_params['xi']}, rho={heston_params['rho']}")
    print(f"{'K':>8}{'Heston FFT price':>18}")
    for K, hp in zip(test_strikes, fft_prices_heston):
        print(f"{K:>8.1f}{hp:>18.6f}")
    print()

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    print("=" * 70)
    print("Part 3: Merton jump-diffusion model -- FFT pricing")
    print("=" * 70)

    merton_params = dict(sigma=0.15, lam=0.5, mu_j=-0.10, sigma_j=0.20)
    merton_cf = lambda u: merton_char_func(u, S0, r, q, T, **merton_params)
    fft_prices_merton = get_prices_at_strikes(merton_cf, test_strikes, S0, r, q, T)

    print(f"Parameters: sigma={merton_params['sigma']}, lambda={merton_params['lam']}, "
          f"mu_j={merton_params['mu_j']}, sigma_j={merton_params['sigma_j']}")
    print(f"{'K':>8}{'Merton FFT price':>18}")
    for K, mp in zip(test_strikes, fft_prices_merton):
        print(f"{K:>8.1f}{mp:>18.6f}")
    print()

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    print("=" * 70)
    print("Part 4: Comparison of the three models' prices")
    print("=" * 70)
    print(f"{'K':>8}{'BS':>12}{'Heston':>12}{'Merton':>12}")
    for K, bp, hp, mp in zip(test_strikes, analytic_prices_bs, fft_prices_heston, fft_prices_merton):
        print(f"{K:>8.1f}{bp:>12.4f}{hp:>12.4f}{mp:>12.4f}")

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Invert implied vol and plot the vol-smile comparison across the three models
    # (more intuitive than comparing raw prices)
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    from scipy.optimize import brentq

    def implied_vol(price, S0, K, r, q, T):
        def obj(sig):
            return bs_analytic_call(S0, K, r, q, sig, T) - price
        try:
            return brentq(obj, 1e-4, 3.0)
        except ValueError:
            return np.nan

    strikes_fine = np.linspace(70, 135, 40)
    prices_bs_fine = get_prices_at_strikes(bs_cf, strikes_fine, S0, r, q, T)
    prices_heston_fine = get_prices_at_strikes(heston_cf, strikes_fine, S0, r, q, T)
    prices_merton_fine = get_prices_at_strikes(merton_cf, strikes_fine, S0, r, q, T)

    iv_bs = [implied_vol(p, S0, K, r, q, T) for p, K in zip(prices_bs_fine, strikes_fine)]
    iv_heston = [implied_vol(p, S0, K, r, q, T) for p, K in zip(prices_heston_fine, strikes_fine)]
    iv_merton = [implied_vol(p, S0, K, r, q, T) for p, K in zip(prices_merton_fine, strikes_fine)]

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

    axes[0].plot(strikes_fine, prices_bs_fine, label="Black-Scholes", lw=2)
    axes[0].plot(strikes_fine, prices_heston_fine, label="Heston", lw=2)
    axes[0].plot(strikes_fine, prices_merton_fine, label="Merton Jump-Diffusion", lw=2)
    axes[0].axvline(S0, color='gray', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, label='ATM')
    axes[0].set_xlabel("Strike K")
    axes[0].set_ylabel("Call Price")
    axes[0].set_title("Carr-Madan FFT: Call Price Curves")
    axes[0].legend()
    axes[0].grid(alpha=0.3)

    axes[1].plot(strikes_fine, iv_bs, label="Black-Scholes (flat, by construction)", lw=2)
    axes[1].plot(strikes_fine, iv_heston, label="Heston implied vol", lw=2)
    axes[1].plot(strikes_fine, iv_merton, label="Merton implied vol", lw=2)
    axes[1].axvline(S0, color='gray', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, label='ATM')
    axes[1].set_xlabel("Strike K")
    axes[1].set_ylabel("Implied Volatility")
    axes[1].set_title("Implied Volatility Smile Comparison")
    axes[1].legend()
    axes[1].grid(alpha=0.3)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig("/home/claude/carr_madan_comparison.png", dpi=140)
    print("\nFigure saved: carr_madan_comparison.png")

A.2: Heston PFE via QE Scheme and Variance-Bucket FFT Grid

Implements Andersen's QE scheme (Eq. 12), the two-dimensional (tenor × variance) precomputation grid of Section 5.1, PFE/EE aggregation, and reproduces Figure 2.

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
"""
Computing PFE (Potential Future Exposure) with Heston + Carr-Madan FFT
======================================================================

Design highlights (corresponding to the architecture discussed earlier):
  1. Use a set of "god's-eye" true Heston parameters (the implied source of a
     synthetic vol surface), skipping the calibration step; the same parameter
     set is used both for path simulation (a simplified stand-in for the P
     measure) and for node valuation (the Q measure).
  2. Jointly simulate (S_t, v_t) using the Andersen (2008) QE scheme with
     martingale correction, because this parameter set violates the Feller
     condition and naive Euler discretization would produce negative variance.
  3. Heston's state is not just S_t but also v_t -- so unlike BS-PFE, we cannot
     bucket on tau alone; we must build a two-dimensional (tau, v) grid, precompute
     one FFT price curve per grid point, and at each node use bilinear interpolation
     (moneyness dimension + variance dimension) instead of repricing from scratch.
  4. Use "slow, path-by-path repricing" as the ground truth to validate the
     accuracy of the bucket + interpolation scheme.

Position: a single long European call, K=100, option maturity T_option=1.25y
Risk horizon: quarterly nodes t = 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0
  (corresponding to remaining tenors tau = 1.0, 0.75, 0.5, 0.25, all > 0)
"""

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '/home/claude')
from carr_madan_fft_en import heston_char_func, carr_madan_fft_call

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 0. Global parameters: market data + "god's-eye" true Heston parameters
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
S0, r, q = 100.0, 0.03, 0.0
K_option = 100.0           # option strike (ATM at inception)
T_option = 1.25             # option maturity
risk_horizon_nodes = np.array([0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0])   # quarterly PFE observation nodes

# True Heston parameters (used both to generate the implied dynamics of the
# "synthetic vol surface" and directly for P-measure path simulation)
v0, kappa, theta, xi, rho = 0.04, 2.0, 0.04, 0.5, -0.7

print("=" * 70)
print("Step 0: Parameter setup and Feller condition check")
print("=" * 70)
feller_lhs, feller_rhs = 2 * kappa * theta, xi ** 2
print(f"v0={v0}, kappa={kappa}, theta={theta}, xi={xi}, rho={rho}")
print(f"2*kappa*theta = {feller_lhs:.4f}  vs  xi^2 = {feller_rhs:.4f}")
print(f"Feller condition {'satisfied' if feller_lhs >= feller_rhs else 'violated -- must use the QE scheme instead of naive Euler'}\n")


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Andersen (2008) QE scheme: joint simulation of (S_t, v_t)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
def simulate_heston_qe(S0, v0, r, q, kappa, theta, xi, rho, T, n_steps, n_paths, seed=42):
    """
    Quadratic-Exponential scheme + Martingale Correction (gamma1=gamma2=0.5, "central" scheme)
    Returns the full paths S_path, v_path, both of shape (n_paths, n_steps+1)
    """
    dt = T / n_steps
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)

    lnS = np.full(n_paths, np.log(S0))
    v = np.full(n_paths, v0)

    lnS_path = np.zeros((n_paths, n_steps + 1))
    v_path = np.zeros((n_paths, n_steps + 1))
    lnS_path[:, 0] = lnS
    v_path[:, 0] = v

    psi_c = 1.5
    exp_kdt = np.exp(-kappa * dt)
    gamma1 = gamma2 = 0.5

    # Martingale-correction coefficients (ensure E[S_{t+dt}|F_t] = S_t*exp((r-q)dt) holds exactly)
    K0 = -rho * kappa * theta * dt / xi
    K1 = gamma1 * dt * (kappa * rho / xi - 0.5) - rho / xi
    K2 = gamma2 * dt * (kappa * rho / xi - 0.5) + rho / xi
    K3 = gamma1 * dt * (1 - rho ** 2)
    K4 = gamma2 * dt * (1 - rho ** 2)

    for i in range(n_steps):
        vt = v.copy()
        m = theta + (vt - theta) * exp_kdt
        s2 = (vt * xi ** 2 * exp_kdt / kappa) * (1 - exp_kdt) + theta * xi ** 2 / (2 * kappa) * (1 - exp_kdt) ** 2
        psi = s2 / np.maximum(m, 1e-12) ** 2

        v_next = np.zeros(n_paths)
        Zv = rng.standard_normal(n_paths)
        Uv = rng.random(n_paths)

        # Branch 1: psi <= psi_c, use the noncentral chi-square approximation (quadratic form of a Gaussian)
        mask1 = psi <= psi_c
        if np.any(mask1):
            psi1 = psi[mask1]
            b2 = 2 / psi1 - 1 + np.sqrt(2 / psi1) * np.sqrt(np.maximum(2 / psi1 - 1, 0))
            a = m[mask1] / (1 + b2)
            v_next[mask1] = a * (np.sqrt(b2) + Zv[mask1]) ** 2

        # Branch 2: psi > psi_c, use the exponential approximation with an atom at zero
        mask2 = ~mask1
        if np.any(mask2):
            psi2 = psi[mask2]
            p = (psi2 - 1) / (psi2 + 1)
            beta = (1 - p) / np.maximum(m[mask2], 1e-12)
            u = Uv[mask2]
            val = np.where(u <= p, 0.0, np.log((1 - p) / np.maximum(1 - u, 1e-12)) / beta)
            v_next[mask2] = val

        v_next = np.maximum(v_next, 0.0)

        Zs = rng.standard_normal(n_paths)  # independent of Zv -- correlation is already
                                            # captured implicitly through v_t, v_next in K1, K2
        lnS_next = lnS + (r - q) * dt + K0 + K1 * vt + K2 * v_next + \
                   np.sqrt(np.maximum(K3 * vt + K4 * v_next, 0.0)) * Zs

        lnS = lnS_next
        v = v_next
        lnS_path[:, i + 1] = lnS
        v_path[:, i + 1] = v

    return np.exp(lnS_path), v_path


print("=" * 70)
print("Step 1: Jointly simulate (S_t, v_t) paths with the QE scheme")
print("=" * 70)

n_paths = 3000
n_steps = 252  # 1 year, daily steps
sim_horizon = 1.0

S_paths, v_paths = simulate_heston_qe(S0, v0, r, q, kappa, theta, xi, rho,
                                       sim_horizon, n_steps, n_paths, seed=42)

E_ST = S_paths[:, -1].mean()
theo_fwd = S0 * np.exp((r - q) * sim_horizon)
print(f"n_paths={n_paths}, n_steps={n_steps} (daily)")
print(f"Martingale check: simulated E[S_T]={E_ST:.4f} vs. theoretical forward={theo_fwd:.4f}, "
      f"relative error={abs(E_ST-theo_fwd)/theo_fwd:.4%}")
print(f"Variance paths: min={v_paths.min():.6f}, max={v_paths.max():.4f} (non-negative throughout: {not np.any(v_paths<0)})\n")


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Variance-bucket grid + FFT precomputation table
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
print("=" * 70)
print("Step 2: Build the (tau, v) 2D grid and precompute the FFT moneyness curve table")
print("=" * 70)

time_idx_nodes = (risk_horizon_nodes * n_steps / sim_horizon).astype(int)  # simulation step index for each quarterly node
tau_grid = T_option - risk_horizon_nodes   # remaining option tenor at each node

# Variance bucket grid: set from the empirical range of the simulated v paths
# (more principled than a hand-picked range)
v_obs_at_nodes = v_paths[:, time_idx_nodes]
v_lo = max(v_obs_at_nodes.min() * 0.5, 1e-4)
v_hi = v_obs_at_nodes.max() * 1.3
n_v_buckets = 12
v_grid = np.linspace(v_lo, v_hi, n_v_buckets)

print(f"Quarterly nodes: {risk_horizon_nodes}, corresponding option tenor tau: {np.round(tau_grid,3)}")
print(f"Variance bucket grid ({n_v_buckets} points): [{v_lo:.4f}, {v_hi:.4f}]")

# Precomputed table: fft_table[(tau_index, v_index)] = (strikes_grid, calls_grid)
fft_table = {}
for ti, tau in enumerate(tau_grid):
    for vi, v_b in enumerate(v_grid):
        cf = lambda u, tau=tau, v_b=v_b: heston_char_func(u, S0, r, q, tau, v_b, kappa, theta, xi, rho)
        strikes_grid, calls_grid = carr_madan_fft_call(cf, S0, r, q, tau)
        order = np.argsort(strikes_grid)
        fft_table[(ti, vi)] = (strikes_grid[order], calls_grid[order])

print(f"Precomputation complete: {len(tau_grid)} tenor buckets x {n_v_buckets} variance buckets = "
      f"{len(tau_grid)*n_v_buckets} FFT calls (each O(N log N)), amortized once across all paths\n")


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Node valuation: bilinear interpolation (variance dimension + strike/moneyness dimension)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
def price_from_bucket_table(ti, S_vec, v_vec, K):
    """
    For a given tenor bucket ti, price a batch of paths' (S_vec, v_vec) states by:
      1) homogeneity relation: C(S_p,K,tau,v) = (S_p/S0_ref) * C(S0_ref, K*S0_ref/S_p, tau, v)
         -- the FFT table was computed at reference spot=S0(=100); each path's actual
         spot differs, so the target K must be converted to its "equivalent K under the
         reference spot" and the resulting price scaled back.
      2) variance dimension: locate the two nearest buckets in v_grid, linearly interpolate
      3) moneyness dimension: linearly interpolate the equivalent K on each bucket's FFT price curve
    Fully vectorized; no per-path FFT calls.
    """
    v_vec_clipped = np.clip(v_vec, v_grid[0], v_grid[-1])
    vi_hi = np.searchsorted(v_grid, v_vec_clipped, side='right')
    vi_hi = np.clip(vi_hi, 1, n_v_buckets - 1)
    vi_lo = vi_hi - 1

    v_lo_arr = v_grid[vi_lo]
    v_hi_arr = v_grid[vi_hi]
    w_hi = np.where(v_hi_arr > v_lo_arr, (v_vec_clipped - v_lo_arr) / (v_hi_arr - v_lo_arr), 0.0)

    scaled_K_vec = K * S0 / S_vec   # convert to the equivalent strike under the reference spot=S0 grid

    prices_ref = np.zeros_like(S_vec)
    for vi in range(n_v_buckets):
        strikes_grid, calls_grid = fft_table[(ti, vi)]
        p_at_scaledK = np.interp(scaled_K_vec, strikes_grid, calls_grid)
        is_lo = (vi_lo == vi)
        is_hi = (vi_hi == vi)
        prices_ref += np.where(is_lo, p_at_scaledK * (1 - w_hi), 0.0)
        prices_ref += np.where(is_hi, p_at_scaledK * w_hi, 0.0)

    return prices_ref * (S_vec / S0)   # scale back to the price at the actual spot


print("=" * 70)
print("Step 3: Value every path at every node via bucket + interpolation (fast version)")
print("=" * 70)

exposure_matrix = np.zeros((n_paths, len(risk_horizon_nodes)))
for ti in range(len(risk_horizon_nodes)):
    step_idx = time_idx_nodes[ti]
    S_at_node = S_paths[:, step_idx]
    v_at_node = v_paths[:, step_idx]
    prices = price_from_bucket_table(ti, S_at_node, v_at_node, K_option)
    exposure_matrix[:, ti] = np.maximum(prices, 0.0)   # a long call price is always positive;
                                                        # max(.,0) is kept here for generality

print("Node valuation complete (all paths vectorized via interpolation, no per-path FFT re-pricing)\n")


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. Slow path-by-path validation (sampled comparison)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
print("=" * 70)
print("Step 4: Slow path-by-path repricing to validate the accuracy of the bucket + interpolation scheme")
print("=" * 70)

n_validate = 60   # number of sampled validation paths (individual per-path FFT calls are
                   # expensive, so only a sample is validated)
rng_val = np.random.default_rng(0)
val_idx = rng_val.choice(n_paths, size=n_validate, replace=False)

max_abs_err = 0.0
errs = []
rel_errs = []
for ti in range(len(risk_horizon_nodes)):
    step_idx = time_idx_nodes[ti]
    tau = tau_grid[ti]
    for p_idx in val_idx:
        S_p = S_paths[p_idx, step_idx]
        v_p = v_paths[p_idx, step_idx]
        # Slow version: build the characteristic function directly from the path's actual
        # (S_p, v_p) and rerun the FFT from scratch; no homogeneity scaling is used here --
        # this is the "ground truth" against which the fast version's interpolation error is checked
        cf_slow = lambda u, S_p=S_p, v_p=v_p, tau=tau: heston_char_func(u, S_p, r, q, tau, v_p, kappa, theta, xi, rho)
        strikes_slow, calls_slow = carr_madan_fft_call(cf_slow, S_p, r, q, tau)
        order = np.argsort(strikes_slow)
        price_slow = np.interp(K_option, strikes_slow[order], calls_slow[order])
        price_fast = exposure_matrix[p_idx, ti]
        err = abs(price_slow - price_fast)
        errs.append(err)
        rel_errs.append(err / max(price_slow, 1e-6))
        max_abs_err = max(max_abs_err, err)

errs = np.array(errs)
rel_errs = np.array(rel_errs)
print(f"Sampled {n_validate} paths x {len(risk_horizon_nodes)} nodes = {len(errs)} comparisons")
print(f"Mean absolute error: {errs.mean():.4f}  |  Median absolute error: {np.median(errs):.4f}")
print(f"Max absolute error: {max_abs_err:.4f}")
print(f"Median relative error: {np.median(rel_errs):.3%}  "
      f"(note: the mean/max relative error is inflated by samples that are deep out-of-the-money "
      f"with short tenor and near-zero price in the denominator, and is not representative; "
      f"the median is the more reliable statistic)")
print("(bucket + interpolation scheme vs. path-by-path FFT ground truth; increase n_v_buckets "
      "for higher accuracy if needed)\n")


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 5. PFE aggregation: 97.5% quantile exposure + expected exposure (EE)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
print("=" * 70)
print("Step 5: PFE aggregation (97.5% quantile exposure + expected exposure EE)")
print("=" * 70)

quantile_level = 0.975
PFE_profile = np.quantile(exposure_matrix, quantile_level, axis=0)
EE_profile = exposure_matrix.mean(axis=0)

print(f"{'Node t':>8}{'Tenor tau':>14}{'PFE(97.5%)':>14}{'EE(mean)':>12}")
for t_i, tau_i, pfe_i, ee_i in zip(risk_horizon_nodes, tau_grid, PFE_profile, EE_profile):
    print(f"{t_i:>8.2f}{tau_i:>14.2f}{pfe_i:>14.4f}{ee_i:>12.4f}")


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 6. Figures: all simulated paths (S_t, v_t) + PFE curve
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
time_axis = np.linspace(0, sim_horizon, n_steps + 1)
n_plot_paths = 300   # plotting all 3,000 paths would be an unreadable smear; a sample of
                      # 300 shows the shape, while the statistics still use all paths

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 10))

# Top-left: all simulated S_t paths (a sample shown)
ax = axes[0, 0]
plot_idx = np.random.default_rng(1).choice(n_paths, n_plot_paths, replace=False)
for idx in plot_idx:
    ax.plot(time_axis, S_paths[idx], lw=0.4, alpha=0.35, color='steelblue')
ax.plot(time_axis, S_paths.mean(axis=0), color='black', lw=2, label='Mean path')
ax.plot(time_axis, np.quantile(S_paths, 0.975, axis=0), color='red', lw=1.3, ls='--', label='97.5% quantile')
ax.plot(time_axis, np.quantile(S_paths, 0.025, axis=0), color='green', lw=1.3, ls='--', label='2.5% quantile')
ax.axhline(K_option, color='gray', ls=':', label=f'K={K_option}')
ax.set_title(f'Heston QE Scheme: Simulated $S_t$ Paths (showing {n_plot_paths}/{n_paths})')
ax.set_xlabel('t (years)')
ax.set_ylabel('$S_t$')
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)

# Top-right: all simulated v_t paths (a sample shown)
ax = axes[0, 1]
for idx in plot_idx:
    ax.plot(time_axis, v_paths[idx], lw=0.4, alpha=0.35, color='darkorange')
ax.plot(time_axis, v_paths.mean(axis=0), color='black', lw=2, label='Mean path')
ax.axhline(theta, color='purple', ls=':', label=f'theta={theta}')
ax.set_title(f'Simulated $v_t$ (Instantaneous Variance) Paths')
ax.set_xlabel('t (years)')
ax.set_ylabel('$v_t$')
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)

# Bottom-left: PFE profile
ax = axes[1, 0]
ax.plot(risk_horizon_nodes, PFE_profile, marker='o', lw=2, color='crimson', label=f'PFE ({quantile_level:.1%})')
ax.plot(risk_horizon_nodes, EE_profile, marker='s', lw=2, color='steelblue', label='EE (mean exposure)')
ax.fill_between(risk_horizon_nodes, EE_profile, PFE_profile, alpha=0.15, color='crimson')
ax.set_title('PFE Profile: Long European Call, K=100 (Heston + FFT)')
ax.set_xlabel('t (years)')
ax.set_ylabel('Exposure')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)

# Bottom-right: error distribution at the validation nodes (bucket interpolation vs. path-by-path FFT)
ax = axes[1, 1]
ax.hist(errs, bins=25, color='slategray', alpha=0.8)
ax.axvline(errs.mean(), color='red', ls='--', label=f'mean={errs.mean():.4f}')
ax.set_title('Bucket-Interpolation vs Ground-Truth FFT: Abs Error Distribution')
ax.set_xlabel('Absolute price error')
ax.set_ylabel('Count')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('/home/claude/pfe_heston_fft.png', dpi=140)
print("\nFigure saved: pfe_heston_fft.png")

A.3: Range Digital Option Pricing

Implements the digital transform of Eq. (11), range digital pricing as a difference of two digitals, and reproduces Figure 3.

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
"""
Part 5: Carr-Madan FFT Pricing of European Range Digital Options
======================================================================

Range digital: payoff = 1{K1 < S_T < K2}, path-independent, depends only on the
terminal distribution. It decomposes into the difference of two cash-or-nothing
digital calls: Range(K1,K2) = D(K1) - D(K2)

The digital call's transform is one order lower than the vanilla call's
(the denominator lacks a (beta+1) factor):
    zeta(u) = exp(-rT) * phi(u - i*alpha) / (alpha + iu)

Structurally this runs exactly parallel to the earlier carr_madan_fft_call,
reusing the same set of phi(u) characteristic functions (BS/Heston/Merton).
"""

import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import norm
from scipy.fft import fft
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '/home/claude')
from carr_madan_fft_en import bs_char_func, heston_char_func, merton_char_func, bs_analytic_call

S0, r, q, T = 100.0, 0.03, 0.0, 1.0


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Carr-Madan FFT pricing engine for digital calls
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
def digital_call_fft(char_func, S0, r, q, T, alpha=1.1, N=2 ** 12, eta=0.25):
    """
    European cash-or-nothing digital call: D(K) = exp(-rT) * Q(S_T > K)
    zeta(u) = exp(-rT) * phi(u - i*alpha) / (alpha + iu)
    """
    lam = 2 * np.pi / (N * eta)
    b = N * lam / 2
    j = np.arange(N)
    u = j * eta

    complex_u = u - alpha * 1j
    phi_val = char_func(complex_u)
    denom = alpha + 1j * u
    zeta = np.exp(-r * T) * phi_val / denom

    simpson_w = (3 + (-1) ** (j + 1) - (j == 0).astype(float)) / 3
    x = np.exp(1j * u * b) * zeta * eta * simpson_w

    fft_vals = fft(x)
    k_grid = -b + lam * j
    digital_prices = (np.exp(-alpha * k_grid) / np.pi) * fft_vals.real
    strikes = np.exp(k_grid)
    return strikes, digital_prices


def digital_prices_at_strikes(char_func, target_strikes, S0, r, q, T, alpha=1.1, N=2 ** 12, eta=0.25):
    strikes_grid, digitals_grid = digital_call_fft(char_func, S0, r, q, T, alpha, N, eta)
    order = np.argsort(strikes_grid)
    return np.interp(target_strikes, strikes_grid[order], digitals_grid[order])


def range_digital_price(char_func, K1, K2, S0, r, q, T, alpha=1.1, N=2 ** 12, eta=0.25):
    """Range(K1,K2) = D(K1) - D(K2), K1 < K2, both points read from the same FFT curve"""
    d1, d2 = digital_prices_at_strikes(char_func, np.array([K1, K2]), S0, r, q, T, alpha, N, eta)
    return d1 - d2


def bs_digital_analytic(S0, K, r, q, sigma, T):
    d2 = (np.log(S0 / K) + (r - q - 0.5 * sigma ** 2) * T) / (sigma * np.sqrt(T))
    return np.exp(-r * T) * norm.cdf(d2)


if __name__ == "__main__":

    print("=" * 70)
    print("Part 5a: Digital call FFT validation (against the closed-form BS formula)")
    print("=" * 70)

    sigma_bs = 0.20
    test_strikes = np.array([70, 80, 90, 100, 110, 120, 130])

    bs_cf = lambda u: bs_char_func(u, S0, r, q, sigma_bs, T)
    fft_digitals = digital_prices_at_strikes(bs_cf, test_strikes, S0, r, q, T)
    analytic_digitals = np.array([bs_digital_analytic(S0, K, r, q, sigma_bs, T) for K in test_strikes])
    max_err = np.max(np.abs(fft_digitals - analytic_digitals))
    print(f"Max absolute error (FFT vs. closed-form BS digital formula): {max_err:.2e}\n")

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    print("=" * 70)
    print("Part 5b: Range digital comparison across three models (fixed-width window, swept around ATM)")
    print("=" * 70)

    heston_params = dict(v0=0.04, kappa=2.0, theta=0.04, xi=0.5, rho=-0.7)
    merton_params = dict(sigma=0.15, lam=0.5, mu_j=-0.10, sigma_j=0.20)

    heston_cf = lambda u: heston_char_func(u, S0, r, q, T, **heston_params)
    merton_cf = lambda u: merton_char_func(u, S0, r, q, T, **merton_params)

    window_width = 10.0   # fixed range width of 10 (e.g., [95,105], [100,110], ...)
    centers = np.arange(75, 126, 5)   # window center swept from 75 to 125

    range_prices_bs = np.array([
        range_digital_price(bs_cf, c - window_width/2, c + window_width/2, S0, r, q, T) for c in centers
    ])
    range_prices_heston = np.array([
        range_digital_price(heston_cf, c - window_width/2, c + window_width/2, S0, r, q, T) for c in centers
    ])
    range_prices_merton = np.array([
        range_digital_price(merton_cf, c - window_width/2, c + window_width/2, S0, r, q, T) for c in centers
    ])

    print(f"{'Center':>10}{'BS':>12}{'Heston':>12}{'Merton':>12}")
    for c, bp, hp, mp in zip(centers, range_prices_bs, range_prices_heston, range_prices_merton):
        print(f"{c:>10.1f}{bp:>12.5f}{hp:>12.5f}{mp:>12.5f}")

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Comparison plot: range digital price curve + the corresponding vanilla call
    # price curve (illustrates the sensitivity difference)
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    strikes_fine = np.linspace(70, 135, 60)

    from scipy.optimize import brentq
    def implied_vol(price, S0, K, r, q, T):
        def obj(sig):
            return bs_analytic_call(S0, K, r, q, sig, T) - price
        try:
            return brentq(obj, 1e-4, 3.0)
        except ValueError:
            return np.nan

    calls_bs_fine = np.array([bs_analytic_call(S0, K, r, q, sigma_bs, T) for K in strikes_fine])

    from carr_madan_fft_en import carr_madan_fft_call
    strikes_h, calls_h = carr_madan_fft_call(heston_cf, S0, r, q, T)
    strikes_m, calls_m = carr_madan_fft_call(merton_cf, S0, r, q, T)
    calls_heston_fine = np.interp(strikes_fine, np.sort(strikes_h), calls_h[np.argsort(strikes_h)])
    calls_merton_fine = np.interp(strikes_fine, np.sort(strikes_m), calls_m[np.argsort(strikes_m)])

    iv_bs_fine = [implied_vol(p, S0, K, r, q, T) for p, K in zip(calls_bs_fine, strikes_fine)]
    iv_heston_fine = [implied_vol(p, S0, K, r, q, T) for p, K in zip(calls_heston_fine, strikes_fine)]
    iv_merton_fine = [implied_vol(p, S0, K, r, q, T) for p, K in zip(calls_merton_fine, strikes_fine)]

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

    axes[0].plot(strikes_fine, iv_bs_fine, label="BS (flat)", lw=2, color='tab:blue')
    axes[0].plot(strikes_fine, iv_heston_fine, label="Heston", lw=2, color='tab:orange')
    axes[0].plot(strikes_fine, iv_merton_fine, label="Merton Jump", lw=2, color='tab:green')
    axes[0].axvline(S0, color='gray', ls='--', alpha=0.5)
    axes[0].set_xlabel("Strike K")
    axes[0].set_ylabel("Implied Vol")
    axes[0].set_title("Reference: Implied Vol Smile (same 3 models as before)")
    axes[0].legend()
    axes[0].grid(alpha=0.3)

    axes[1].plot(centers, range_prices_bs, marker='o', label="BS", lw=2, color='tab:blue')
    axes[1].plot(centers, range_prices_heston, marker='o', label="Heston", lw=2, color='tab:orange')
    axes[1].plot(centers, range_prices_merton, marker='o', label="Merton Jump", lw=2, color='tab:green')
    axes[1].axvline(S0, color='gray', ls='--', alpha=0.5, label='ATM')
    axes[1].set_xlabel(f"Window center (width={window_width})")
    axes[1].set_ylabel("Range Digital Price")
    axes[1].set_title(f"Range Digital: P({{K-{window_width/2:.0f} < S_T < K+{window_width/2:.0f}}})")
    axes[1].legend()
    axes[1].grid(alpha=0.3)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig("/home/claude/range_digital_comparison.png", dpi=140)
    print("\nFigure saved: range_digital_comparison.png")
